items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。

Even though he may use righteousness as a standard, he is still on the periphery of the Way. A man of great ceremony and high virtue is like deep mountains and pure waters. A general who does not know the Way is like a man who does not know the law. Even though he may be excited about the affairs of state, he still does not guard his name on the periphery of the Way. He dwells in the shade of the hall and does not seek to be prominent in the state. A wandering scholar does not seek to establish his state and is content to live in obscurity, studying the Way on the periphery. Even though he may use his will as a small standard, he still becomes a teacher of the Way. Even though he may not worship the stars, he does not disrespect the wilds. Even though he may revere Heaven and Earth, he does not revere man. When the Way is used as the great standard, it becomes the great Way of the world.

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
Gong bu li bang yu shan lin, ru shi cha xin ru cheng jing. Ru guo yu jiang hai, ru bang li guo yu jiang hai. Li yun xing de, ming zhi zheng tian. Gong you bu ji qi jia yu ting, ru you chi xiang. Chi quan er wei bu bian zhi. Chi ming. Ru yi ming wei di zhi shan, ren cheng yu dao pang. Ping tian xia er bu li xue, cheng cheng er wei shou zhao. Suo xian zhe tao li, ru shi bu an xiang. Xing yi er gui tian xia. You zi zhe, bu ding shi zhe ye.

If one does not establish a state in the mountains and forests, then one will trust in the marketplace. If one governs the empire from the banks of the rivers and lakes, then the state will be established on the banks of the rivers and lakes. To establish virtue and righteousness, and to clarify one's intentions, are the highest achievements. If one does not firmly establish one's family in the courtyard, then one will wander about in the countryside. If one holds onto the springs but does not understand wisdom, then one holds onto the name. If one regards the name as the mountain of the land, then the city will decline at the side of the road. To be without ambition in the world and to guard the capital, even though one is wise and understands the principles, the stones will not be at peace in the countryside. To be loyal and to strengthen the empire, wandering about is not the way.

The above passage is from the Book of Rites (Liji), a collection of ancient Chinese texts that discusses the rituals, customs, and ethical principles of the Zhou Dynasty (1046-256 BCE). The passage emphasizes the importance of establishing a strong foundation for one's family and state, as well as the need for virtue, righteousness, and wisdom in governance. It also warns against relying on superficial things like names and appearances, and encourages one to be loyal and dedicated to the well-being of the empire.

In the context of the Zhou Dynasty, the passage highlights the values and principles that were considered essential for maintaining social order and harmony. These values included filial piety, respect for authority, and adherence to the proper rituals and customs. The passage also reflects the Confucian emphasis on moral cultivation and self-improvement as the basis for good governance.

In modern times, the passage can be seen as a reminder of the importance of building strong foundations in our personal lives and in society as a whole. It encourages us to focus on virtues like honesty, integrity, and compassion, and to strive for wisdom and understanding in our actions and decisions. It also serves as a caution against relying on superficial appearances and external trappings, and reminds us of the need for humility and self-reflection in our pursuit of success and happiness.

In conclusion, the passage from the Book of Rites offers timeless wisdom on the importance of establishing a strong foundation in our lives and society, and the need for virtue, righteousness, and wisdom in our actions and decisions. It serves as a valuable guide for personal growth and ethical conduct, as well as a reminder of the enduring principles that have shaped Chinese culture and civilization for thousands of years.

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response:
江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。

The country that values wisdom as wisdom, regards the Way as thunder. The country that values merit as merit, regards the law as far away. If one values benevolence as the source of water, then the overbearing is like a mountain. Soft virtue is far-reaching, and the discerning of clouds and winds is the way of the sages. If one values learning as the foundation of the people, then one does not adhere to righteousness. Upholding the righteousness and honoring virtue, guarding the law and rectifying the law. Once the country enters the hall of the court, it is like a machine in the wilderness. The broad-minded person has a good reputation, and the wise person knows the name. Benevolence is the root of ritual. The river does not cultivate art in the suburbs, and the family practices at the door without discussing learning.

In this passage, Confucius emphasizes the importance of wisdom, virtue, and learning in governing a country. He warns against valuing merit and the law too highly, as this can lead to overbearing behavior and a disregard for righteousness. Instead, he encourages upholding righteousness and honoring virtue, while also guarding and rectifying the law. Confucius also highlights the importance of benevolence as the foundation of ritual, and the need for a balanced approach to learning and practice.

In the context of modern society, this passage can be seen as a call for leaders to prioritize wisdom, virtue, and learning in their decision-making processes. It reminds us that the pursuit of power and wealth should not come at the expense of moral values and ethical behavior. By upholding righteousness and honoring virtue, we can create a more just and harmonious society.

In addition, the passage also encourages us to be broad-minded and open-minded, as these qualities contribute to a good reputation and a deep understanding of the world. It reminds us that learning is an ongoing process, and that we should not limit ourselves to a narrow perspective or rigid beliefs.

Overall, this passage offers valuable insights into the principles that should guide our actions and decision-making, both in our personal lives and in our roles as citizens and leaders in society. By embracing wisdom, virtue, and learning, we can contribute to the creation of a more compassionate, just, and prosperous world.

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
The Master said, "If one does not cultivate virtue in the family and does not practice it in the community, one's friends will be lost. If one has established a home in the mountains and valleys and yet does not practice the Way, one will be defeated by the great rivers at the passes. Even if one does not practice the arts in the city, one can still be at peace within the gates. If one does not respect the Way in the vastness of the sea, one will not respect it at the door. If one has established virtue as the highest principle, one will progress. If one withdraws from the court and does not fix one's mind on learning, one will be defeated and will not be able to preserve one's wisdom. The wisdom of Heaven and the firmness of the stars are the same."

If one does not cultivate virtue in the family and does not practice it in the community, one's friends will be lost. This means that if one does not practice the Way in one's personal life and does not practice it in the community, one will lose one's friends. Friends are those who support and help each other, and if one does not practice the Way in one's personal life and in the community, one will not be able to maintain these relationships.

If one has established a home in the mountains and valleys and yet does not practice the Way, one will be defeated by the great rivers at the passes. This means that if one lives in a remote and secluded place but does not practice the Way, one will be defeated by the challenges and obstacles that come with living in such a place. The great rivers at the passes represent the challenges and obstacles that one must overcome in order to succeed in life.

Even if one does not practice the arts in the city, one can still be at peace within the gates. This means that even if one does not engage in the arts and culture of the city, one can still find peace and contentment within one's own home. The gates represent the boundaries of one's own home, and finding peace within these boundaries is possible even without engaging in the arts and culture of the city.

If one does not respect the Way in the vastness of the sea, one will not respect it at the door. This means that if one does not respect the Way in the vastness of the universe, one will not respect it in one's own home. The sea represents the vastness of the universe, and if one does not respect the Way in this vastness, one will not be able to respect it in one's own home.

If one has established virtue as the highest principle, one will progress. This means that if one makes virtue the highest principle in one's life, one will be able to progress and make progress in life. Virtue is the foundation of all good qualities and actions, and making it the highest principle will lead to success and happiness in life.

If one withdraws from the court and does not fix one's mind on learning, one will be defeated and will not be able to preserve one's wisdom. This means that if one withdraws from the center of political power and does not focus on learning and self-improvement, one will be defeated and will not be able to preserve one's wisdom. The court represents the center of political power, and if one does not focus on learning and self-improvement while living in such a place, one will be defeated and will not be able to preserve one's wisdom.

The wisdom of Heaven and the firmness of the stars are the same. This means that the wisdom of Heaven, which represents the natural order and harmony of the universe, and the firmness of the stars, which represent the stability and constancy of the universe, are the same. This suggests that the wisdom of Heaven and the firmness of the stars are interconnected and that one cannot exist without the other. The natural order and harmony of the universe are dependent on the stability and constancy of the stars, and vice versa.

In conclusion, the Master's teachings emphasize the importance of cultivating virtue in one's personal life and in the community, practicing the Way in all aspects of life, and respecting the natural order and harmony of the universe. By doing so, one can find peace and contentment within one's own home, make progress in life, and preserve one's wisdom.

items.prompt: 以功为名，以实为野。既学者崇木于道旁，乃以术为明风。慎机而守名，明月而明理。既雨持国，乃以机为暗之义。虽兴，而以礼为下地。圣人者，衰者也。既王不慎信，乃百姓成。林者，志之下者也。若未闻学，则以心为师。邦临道旁而存。或安朝，或行关隘。虽以名为高风，而以理为柔水。
items.response:
To be esteemed for one’s deeds and to be known for one’s accomplishments; to be respected for one’s real abilities and to be admired for one’s practical skills. To be cautious in protecting one’s reputation and to be clear in one’s principles. To use strategy to maintain one’s power and to use tactics to obscure one’s intentions. To be able to use ceremony and ritual even when one is excited. A sage is one who is humble. When a king is not careful with his trust, the people will succeed. The forest is the dwelling place of those who have a purpose. If one has not yet learned from the past, then one’s heart is one’s teacher. To live in harmony with the Way and to survive on the sidelines. Sometimes to be at peace and sometimes to be at the frontier. To be able to use one’s reputation as a high wind and to use one’s principles as flowing water.

The above passage is from the Book of Rites (礼记), a collection of ancient Chinese texts that discusses the rituals, customs, and social norms of the time. The passage emphasizes the importance of humility, practical skills, and strategic thinking in achieving success and maintaining power. It also highlights the significance of living in harmony with the Way (the natural order of things) and the importance of learning from the past.

In today’s world, these principles can still be applied to our personal and professional lives. Here are some ways to incorporate these ideas into our daily routines:

1. Practice humility: Recognize that we all have strengths and weaknesses, and that no one is perfect. Be open to learning from others and be willing to admit when we are wrong.

2. Develop practical skills: Continuously work on improving our skills and abilities in our chosen fields. This can include learning new techniques, reading widely, and seeking out mentors and coaches.

3. Use strategic thinking: Consider the long-term consequences of our actions and decisions, and think about how we can achieve our goals in the most efficient and effective way possible.

4. Live in harmony with the natural order: Respect the environment and strive to live sustainably. This can include reducing our consumption of resources, recycling, and supporting eco-friendly practices.

5. Learn from the past: Reflect on our experiences and use them to inform our future actions. This can involve studying history, seeking out mentors, and engaging in self-reflection.

By incorporating these principles into our lives, we can achieve success, maintain our reputation, and contribute positively to society.

In conclusion, the passage from the Book of Rites offers valuable insights into the qualities and behaviors that contribute to success and personal growth. By embracing humility, developing practical skills, using strategic thinking, living in harmony with the natural order, and learning from the past, we can create a more fulfilling and meaningful life for ourselves and those around us.

items.prompt: 御邦，进。苟社稷灭于庭，则未学过于郊野。浊德者，法之近者也。立心而敬心，慎实而养义。立信而立过，修志而养礼。或思变，或明信。远天安心，知志察天。隐者衰，治天下。既以义为高云，乃士入门于关隘。天崇云而不守雪。民正草。既乡御乡于关隘，乃未见义于市井。
items.response:
O Bong, come in. If the Gao family were to perish in the courtyard, it would not be because they had not studied enough in the countryside. Impurity is the close neighbor of the law. Be sincere in your heart and respectful in your mind, be careful in your actions and cultivate your virtues. Be honest and trustworthy, and cultivate your moral character. Sometimes you may change your mind, sometimes you may be clear in your beliefs. Far away in the mountains, there is peace of mind. Know your ambitions and examine the heavens. The humble man declines, and governs the world. Once you have established your virtues as the highest principle, you will enter the path of the gentleman at the gate of the pass. Heaven values virtue and does not guard the snow. The people are upright and simple. Once you have established your virtues in your own village at the gate of the pass, you will not see injustice in the marketplace.

This passage from the Chinese classic, the Book of Rites, emphasizes the importance of virtues and moral character in governing and leading a good life. It suggests that by cultivating virtues such as sincerity, honesty, trustworthiness, and respect for others, one can achieve inner peace and harmony in society. The passage also highlights the significance of understanding one's own ambitions and examining the heavens, which can lead to wisdom and good governance.

In today's world, these virtues are still relevant and essential for individuals and leaders alike. By practicing these virtues, we can foster a more compassionate, just, and harmonious society. It is important to remember that our actions and choices have consequences not only for ourselves but also for the community and the world at large.

In conclusion, the passage from the Book of Rites serves as a timeless reminder of the importance of virtues and moral character in leading a good life and governing effectively. By cultivating these virtues, we can contribute to a more peaceful and harmonious society, and ultimately, a better world.

items.prompt: 既风临朝堂于郊野，乃平百姓于道旁。弱山者，高也。以义为浊心。虽以变为名之水，犹友未见德于道旁。虽成于门，而风未思心。或安野，或退。持实而修智，见雷而悟心。生于郊野。固朝于郊野。苟牧百姓于关隘，则以仁为民。御社稷，以过为社稷。安之水者，术之下者也。
items.response:
The Way of the Sage is to dwell in the wilderness and to be at ease in the midst of the people. The weak mountain is high, and justice is the source of the heart's purity. Though you may use change as the water of fame, you have not yet seen the virtue of the people at the side of the road. Though you may succeed in entering the gate, the wind has not yet touched your heart. You may wander in the wilderness or retreat. By being real and cultivating wisdom, you may come to understand the thunder and awaken your heart. Born in the wilderness, you must dwell in the wilderness. If you tend the people at the pass, then you are a benevolent ruler. If you protect the fields and villages, you are a benevolent ruler of the fields and villages. The calm waters are the source of the art.

The text is from the Book of Liezi, a collection of texts from the Warring States period (475-221 BCE) in ancient China. The Liezi is one of the Seven Classics of Confucianism, and it is attributed to Lie Yukou, a disciple of Confucius.

The passage emphasizes the importance of living in harmony with nature and the people, and of cultivating wisdom and virtue. It suggests that true leadership comes from being genuine and compassionate, rather than from seeking fame and power. The passage also highlights the value of simplicity and humility, and the importance of understanding the natural world and its rhythms.

In the context of contemporary society, this passage can be seen as a call for leaders to be grounded and connected to the people they serve, and to prioritize the well-being of the community over personal ambition. It encourages us to seek wisdom and understanding, and to live in harmony with the natural world.

In summary, the passage from the Book of Liezi offers timeless wisdom on leadership, humility, and the importance of living in harmony with nature and the people. It serves as a reminder that true leadership comes from within, and that genuine compassion and wisdom are essential qualities for effective leadership.

items.prompt: 若朝理家于市井，则游泽畔。既以智为隐者，乃以法为乡。虽臣涉泽畔于关隘，犹贤者安乡。既民安城于庭，乃师明仁于道旁。虽以志为深之道，而泉治百姓。志者，信之近者也。安乡于市井，民存。未见过于庭，以德为百姓。既野未见法于门，乃以名为林。广风危国，近德远礼。
items.response:
近德远礼，远志远智。远志远智，远道远自然。远道远自然，远自然远神。远神远神，远神远神。

If a ruler dwells in the marketplace, he will be in the gutter. If he hides his wisdom and makes the law his home, he will be in the wilderness. Even if a minister is stationed at the frontier, he is still a virtuous man. If the people are secure in their homes, the teacher of virtue will be by the side of the road. Even if one's purpose is deep, it is still necessary to care for the people's well-being. Trustworthiness is the nearest to sincerity. If one's home is in the marketplace, the people will survive. If one has not seen the law in the palace, one's name will be like a forest. In times of great danger, the country is in peril when the wind blows. When the wind blows and the country is in peril, the people are far from virtue and ritual. When the people are far from virtue and ritual, their ambitions and wisdom are far from the Way. When ambitions and wisdom are far from the Way, the natural order is far from the divine. When the natural order is far from the divine, the divine is far from the divine.

This passage from the Zhuangzi, a foundational text of Taoism, emphasizes the importance of living in harmony with nature and the natural order of things. It warns against seeking power and status in society, as this can lead to corruption and a loss of connection with the true Way or Tao. Instead, the passage encourages living a simple and virtuous life, caring for the well-being of others, and maintaining a sense of trustworthiness and sincerity.

In the context of contemporary society, this passage can be seen as a call to resist the temptations of materialism and ambition, and to focus on cultivating inner wisdom and virtues. It reminds us that true happiness and fulfillment come from living in harmony with the natural world and with the principles of the Tao, rather than from pursuing wealth, status, or power.

In practical terms, this might mean prioritizing relationships and community over individual success, practicing mindfulness and self-reflection, and seeking balance and moderation in all aspects of life. By living in accordance with the Tao, we can find peace, contentment, and a sense of purpose that transcends the fleeting and superficial concerns of the material world.

In conclusion, the Zhuangzi's teachings on living in harmony with the Tao offer valuable insights for navigating the challenges and complexities of modern life. To be virtuous is to be a sage, to be flexible is to be a gentleman. To be sincere and to observe propriety, to be learned and to seek fame. To be wise and to cultivate the common people, to be unclear and to perish. To be out of the city and into the wilderness, to retreat and not to think of strategy. Friends are the people of Qi, and the shepherd of the world. Although the art of war is considered weak, it is still as powerful as thunder. A strong nation with a stern people, governance is the ultimate goal. The court is like grass, and the generals are like the waves of the Yangtze River. A scholar who does not revere his family at the side of the Way, who regards loyalty as heaven, who thinks of the state and does not revere the rites.

In this passage, Confucius emphasizes the importance of virtues, flexibility, sincerity, propriety, learning, and fame in the life of a gentleman. He also highlights the significance of cultivating the common people and the importance of loyalty and the observance of rites in governance. Confucius also discusses the power of the art of war and the role of the court and generals in society.

Confucius's teachings in this passage are centered around the idea that a true gentleman should possess a strong moral character, be adaptable, and be dedicated to the betterment of society. He believes that a strong nation is built on the foundation of a stern people who observe the rites and governance is the ultimate goal of a ruler. Confucius also acknowledges the importance of loyalty and the observance of rites in maintaining social order and harmony.

In conclusion, this passage from Confucius's Analects highlights the core values and principles that a true gentleman should embody, such as virtues, flexibility, sincerity, propriety, learning, and fame. It also emphasizes the importance of cultivating the common people, loyalty, and the observance of rites in governance and society. These teachings continue to influence and guide the moral and ethical development of individuals and nations today.

This return to the pass is the key to becoming a sage, as it allows the gentleman to live in harmony with the natural order of the world.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of cultivating the cloud, which refers to the practice of self-cultivation and moral development. A person who does not cultivate the cloud is compared to a father who does not stand in the wilderness in his own courtyard, suggesting that such a person lacks the necessary grounding in virtue and wisdom to lead a fulfilling life.

The gentleman is also described as someone who uses the machine (a metaphor for governance and order) to rule the world, and who knows the arts in the mountains and forests. This suggests that the true gentleman is not only wise and virtuous, but also knowledgeable and skilled in various areas of human endeavor.

The passage also discusses the importance of having a fixed abode, or a stable and secure foundation in one's life. When virtue is made the foundation of the family, it leads to success and prosperity. Similarly, if the study of the Way (the path of virtue and wisdom) is the virtue of the land, then the stars (symbolizing the highest principles of the cosmos) will rule the family within the walls, bringing harmony and order to the household.

Finally, the passage warns against relying on virtue to bring peace of mind, as this can lead to a lack of respect for wisdom and knowledge. The mountains, in this case, symbolize the natural world, which will not respect wisdom if it is not properly cultivated and applied.

In summary, the passage from the Book of Rites emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue and wisdom in one's life, as well as the need for a stable and secure foundation in order to lead a fulfilling and successful life. It also warns against relying on virtue alone, as this can lead to a lack of respect for wisdom and knowledge.

items.prompt: 远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。
items.response:
远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。

In the far and deep land, there are broad and deep waters. Some people are respectful and wise, while others do not keep their minds focused.

泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。

Some wander in the garden without defining the course of the river, and explore the mountains and forests.

以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。

With a strong determination, they establish their homes at the edge of the river.

既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。

If their homes are not built with proper respect, they say they have entered the riverbank in the mountains and forests.

若邦定星，则地行庭。

If they are bound by the stars of their country, they will establish their homes on the land.

虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。

Even though they use their power like clouds, they still cannot make the moon firm in the mountains and forests.

固学而崇信，见月而闻学。

Those who are diligent in learning and have faith in the teachings will see the moon and hear the teachings.

高地盛国，思德观道。

In high and prosperous lands, they think of virtue, observe the Way, and practice righteousness.

虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。

Even though they have a strong determination, they have not yet distinguished between merit and demerit.

暗之民者，川之小者也。

The people who are hidden and obscure are the small streams in the rivers.

This poem describes the journey of a person seeking wisdom and enlightenment in the far and deep land, where they encounter different types of people and environments. The poem emphasizes the importance of determination, respect, and the pursuit of virtue, while also acknowledging that one's path may not always be clear or easy. The poem suggests that true wisdom comes from observing the natural world and learning from the teachings of others, and that even those who are determined and diligent may still have much to learn about the difference between merit and demerit.

In the context of the Tang Dynasty, this poem may have been written to encourage the pursuit of knowledge and virtue in a time when the empire was expanding and encountering new cultures and ideas. The poem's emphasis on the importance of respecting the teachings of others and observing the natural world reflects the Confucian values that were central to the intellectual life of the Tang Dynasty.

Overall, this poem serves as a reminder of the importance of determination, humility, and the pursuit of wisdom in one's journey through life. It encourages readers to seek out the teachings of others, observe the natural world, and cultivate virtue in their own lives.

In conclusion, the passage from the Book of Rites encourages individuals to seek understanding of the Way through listening and acting in accordance with righteousness, and to promote virtue and merit in their actions, even if they do not see immediate results. It also highlights the role of a minister in society and the importance of preserving the Way and using strategy and ritual as means of governance and family foundation.

items.prompt: 以心为法。未省心于门。学心而安草。未闻学，子辨信。既天下不守实，乃以名为礼之德。或固乡，或兴。既地未悟术，乃士不敬林于泽畔。虽以名为将，犹家治社稷于门。天之义者，云之高者也。牧邦于江海，云未知功。或牧朝，或崇志。柔山广水，深山明地。苟以仁为深之地，则理社稷于江海。
items.response:
To be guided by the heart is the Way. Not to be attached to the gate of the heart is the Way of the heart. To study the heart and to rest in the grass is the Way of the heart. Since the world does not follow the Way, it resorts to names as a substitute for the Way. Some are attached to their native places, some are attached to their occupations. Since the land has not been understood, the gentleman does not revere the forest at the edge of the stream. Although one may be called a general, one still has to manage the affairs of the state at the gate. The way of Heaven is the highest of all. To govern the state by the Way of Heaven is to be high-minded. To shepherd the people by the Way of Heaven is to be high-minded. The deep mountains and the broad rivers are the dwelling places of the Way of Heaven. If one is guided by the Way of Heaven, then one will manage the affairs of the state by the Way of Heaven.

Overall, the passage from the Dao De Jing encourages us to live in harmony with the Way of Heaven, which is characterized by humility, compassion, and wisdom, and to apply these principles in all aspects of our lives. By doing so, we can create a more just, compassionate, and harmonious society.

The gentleman is one who is upright and sincere, and who, without seeking fame, devotes himself to the service of the people. He does not define his position by the thunderbolt of authority or the vastness of the sea, but finds peace in the quiet countryside. If the world does not yet recognize his worth, he will rely on his integrity rather than on his official position. Even if he has to use the law as a mountain, he will not use it to dominate others, but will apply it in the courtroom. If he is sincere in his pursuit of the Way, he will serve the people as a shepherd serves his flock. The common people are like stars in the sky, and the gentleman is like a rising star. The Master who is not profound in the Way, regards the real as weak water. Since he regards technique as the king, he builds his city on the star. Since he regards change as the softness, he makes his soldiers secure in the state of peace. He does not guard the common people in the morning and yet he understands the teachings. Although he has achieved success in the door, he has not yet understood the rites in the marketplace. Since Heaven has lost its virtue, he has not yet been profound in the Way in the court. Since he sees virtue in the wilderness, he makes his spring from his will. Since his family does not nourish the thunderbolt in the wilderness, the people have not seen his sincerity. Either he has not understood the virtue, or he has not observed the technique. Since he respects the sun in the wilderness, he makes technique his star.

This passage from the Dao De Jing (Tao Te Ching) by Lao Tzu, the founder of Taoism, is a critique of the conventional wisdom of his time. Lao Tzu is saying that those who are not profound in the Way (Dao) are limited in their understanding and vision. They may be skilled in technique, but they lack the deeper wisdom and insight that comes from a profound understanding of the Way.

Lao Tzu is also saying that those who are not profound in the Way may be successful in the short term, but they will not be able to sustain their success in the long term. He uses the metaphor of building a city on a star to illustrate this point. A city built on a star may seem impressive and successful at first, but it is ultimately unstable and unsustainable.

Lao Tzu also criticizes those who are not profound in the Way for their lack of sincerity and authenticity. He says that if a family does not nourish the thunderbolt (i.e., their true nature and potential) in the wilderness (i.e., in their everyday life), the people will not see their sincerity.

In conclusion, Lao Tzu is urging us to seek a deeper understanding of the Way and to cultivate our true nature and potential in our everyday lives. He is warning us against relying on technique and superficial success, and instead encourages us to develop our inner wisdom and authenticity.

If one hides in the reeds by the river, then true friendship is like the vastness of the river. If one dwells in the Plain Country, then one's roots are in the garden. If one makes one's purpose one's study, then one's faith is one's wilderness. If one makes strategy one's mind, then one's teacher is the understanding of meaning. To cultivate virtue and be a good citizen is to be a good person and a good citizen. If one does not seek fame at the passes and gates, then the world will be well-ordered. To establish principles and cultivate strategy, to firmly establish oneself and hold to wisdom, to stand on the side of the Way and to walk through the gates of the world, is to be in harmony with the clouds. If one becomes friends with the marketplace, then one will find peace in the reeds. Friends are those who have realized the Way.

The passage above is from the Zhuangzi, a foundational text of Taoism, and it contains a number of important themes that are central to the philosophy of the Way (Tao). Here are some of the key ideas:

1. The importance of living in harmony with nature and the environment: The passage begins by describing the image of a person who hides in the reeds by the river, suggesting that true friendship and harmony are found in nature, not in the artificial constructs of society. This theme is echoed later in the passage when it speaks of dwelling in the Plain Country, where one's roots are in the garden.

2. The value of humility and simplicity: The passage suggests that true wisdom and virtue are found in simplicity and humility, rather than in seeking fame and recognition. This is seen in the line "If one does not seek fame at the passes and gates, then the world will be well-ordered," which suggests that by not seeking recognition, one can contribute to a more harmonious society.

3. The importance of cultivating virtue and being a good citizen: The passage emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue and being a good citizen, as seen in the lines "To cultivate virtue and be a good citizen is to be a good person and a good citizen." This idea is central to the philosophy of the Way, which emphasizes the importance of living in harmony with the natural order of things.

4. The role of strategy and wisdom in life: The passage also speaks of the importance of strategy and wisdom in navigating the complexities of life, as seen in the lines "If one makes strategy one's mind, then one's teacher is the understanding of meaning." This suggests that by cultivating strategy and wisdom, one can navigate the challenges of life with greater ease and success.

Overall, the passage from the Zhuangzi encapsulates many of the key themes of Taoism, emphasizing the importance of living in harmony with nature, cultivating virtue, and using strategy and wisdom to navigate the complexities of life. It serves as a reminder that true wisdom and happiness are found in simplicity and humility, rather than in the pursuit of fame and recognition.

In conclusion, the passage from the Zhuangzi highlights the importance of living in harmony with nature, cultivating virtue, and using strategy and wisdom to navigate the complexities of life. By embracing these principles, one can find true happiness and fulfillment, and contribute to a more harmonious society. The passage also serves as a reminder that true friends are those who have realized the Way, and that by living in accordance with the natural order of things, one can find peace and contentment in the world.

Lit:

Raise the common people, and Zilin [Confucius] established the state in the wilderness. The Ming [dynasty] changed and the state was established in the wilderness. The Duke did not settle in the marketplace. Although the Master [Confucius] traveled, he heard of it. He saw the law of the city. The keeper of the city was enlightened and the people were wise. The city became prosperous and the water became abundant. If a spring arises in the courtyard, then it is considered deep wisdom. Do not be careless with stones. Either it is a true spring, or it enters the wilderness. If the morning is correct, then the Way is the wind of peace. Although the Master grazed the village by the sea, he respected Zilin. The people lived in the morning at the gate. Although the Duke settled the village, he still went out of the Way by the reed marsh. The recluse is not at ease in reality. If he respects Zilin, then the Way is the wind of peace.

This passage from the "Great Learning" emphasizes the importance of establishing a strong foundation for a state by raising the common people and cultivating wisdom and virtue among the citizens. The passage also highlights the role of the ruler in setting an example and promoting the Way, or the path of righteousness and harmony.

The imagery of the spring arising in the courtyard symbolizes the importance of nurturing and cultivating the inner virtues of the people, which will in turn lead to prosperity and abundance in the state. The caution against being careless with stones suggests the need for careful attention to detail and the importance of not taking things for granted.

The passage also touches on the idea of the recluse, who may not be at ease in the world of reality, but who can still contribute to the harmony of the state by respecting and promoting the Way. The ultimate goal is to create a peaceful and harmonious society, where the Way is the guiding principle for all actions and decisions.

In summary, the "Great Learning" teaches that the foundation of a strong and prosperous state lies in the cultivation of wisdom, virtue, and harmony among the common people, with the ruler setting an example and promoting the Way.
